burial.plot_notes: Old Trinity Church Cemetery, Brooklyn CT (with husband)

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Maj. Gen. Israel Putnam (Bunker Hill hero); Catherine was the eldest daughter of Col. Putnam.
parents.mother_research_notes: Surname Hutchinson per Catherine's middle name; Mr. Hutchinson described in George Brinley Sr.'s 1856 letter as a fixture by the parlor fireplace, blind and deaf in his old age.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Bunker Hill hero Israel Putnam
narrative_blocks.body: She was the eldest daughter of Col Daniel Putnam — and thus granddaughter of Major General Israel Putnam, the Continental Army officer famous for the order 'Don't fire till you see the whites of their eyes' at Bunker Hill in June 1775. Her middle name Hutchinson preserved her mother's family line in the same gesture.

In 1818 her father Col Daniel Putnam published a defense of Israel Putnam against Henry Dearborn's revisionist history of Bunker Hill. Catherine grew up inside that argument — the Putnam family's effort to control the historical record of the grandfather's reputation.

narrative_blocks.header: Many children died young; only George Jr survived
narrative_blocks.body: She had five known children with George Brinley Sr — Catherine, Harriet, Anne, Sarah Tyler, and George Jr — but only George Jr is attached to the tree as a built entry. Several of her daughters died young, a recurring loss the 1856 Brinley family letter recalled with sorrow.

In 1818 her father Col Daniel Putnam published a defense of Israel Putnam against Henry Dearborn's revisionist history of Bunker Hill. Catherine grew up inside that argument — the Putnam family's effort to control the historical record of the grandfather's reputation.

burial.plot_notes: Old Trinity Church Graveyard, Brooklyn CT — Brinley Memorial to the left and behind the church

career.notes: Succeeded Dr. Dix at the head of a large Boston drug store; built family fortune. (Source: A History of the Putnam Family.)

narrative_blocks.header: Vivid 1856 letter recalled his 1805 Brooklyn wedding
narrative_blocks.body: Fifty years after marrying Catherine Putnam at Brooklyn CT in 1805, eighty-two-year-old George dictated a long letter recalling the scene. The bride's deaf-blind grandfather wore a freshly-powdered wig and bright scarlet suit, leaning over Rev Fogg's shoulder to catch the words — 'a scene for Hogarth's pencil,' he thought.

narrative_blocks.header: Took over Dr Dix's Boston drug store
narrative_blocks.body: For many years he ran one of Boston's largest drug stores, succeeding Dr Dix. The fortune he built there carried the family to Hartford and Brooklyn Connecticut homes by the early 1800s and eventually produced his son George Brinley Jr, one of America's most famous 19th-century book collectors.

narrative_blocks.header: Most of their five children died young
narrative_blocks.body: Of five known children with Catherine Hutchinson Putnam — Catherine, Harriet, Anne, Sarah Tyler, and George Brinley Jr — 'many died young' per the family record. Only George Jr (1817-1875), the future book collector, is documented as reaching adulthood with descendants.

quotes.text: April 30th, 1805 - an event (that) occurred never to be obliterated from my memory. My marriage with Col. Putnam's eldest daughter Catherine. It took place in the West parlor. Why it was not the church, I know not - as it was - what a scene for Hogarth's pencil - all the family of course present - the servants black and white - hired men all congregated in a crowded room.
quotes.attribution: George Brinley Sr., letter Nov 22, 1856 — recollections of his 1805 marriage at Brooklyn CT

sources.label: A History of the Putnam Family

notable.notable_blurb: 19th-century Americana book collector; bequeathed Yale 12,000 volumes
notable.primary_url_label: William L. Clements Library — George Brinley Papers

narrative_blocks.header: Built one of America's greatest Americana libraries
narrative_blocks.body: He spent his life as a Hartford banker but began collecting Americana while still a student. Before his 1875 death he had amassed over 12,000 rare volumes — bequeathed in large part to Yale University, with the remainder auctioned 1878-1893.

narrative_blocks.header: Active in Episcopal Church and Trinity College
narrative_blocks.body: Throughout his life he was active in the Episcopal Church — in his own parish, supporting Trinity College, and in state and national conventions. He died in Hamilton, Bermuda on May 16, 1875, almost certainly while seeking a milder climate for failing health.

sources.label: William L. Clements Library — George Brinley Papers

education.notes: Began collecting Americana while a student.

institutions.institution_blurb: Active Episcopal layman who supported Trinity College throughout his life.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT (with parents and siblings)
West Laurel Hill Cemetery, Bala Cynwyd PA

narrative_blocks.header: 1918-19 flu: turned YWCA building into emergency hospital
narrative_blocks.body: During the 1918-1919 influenza pandemic, Ellen Terry Brinley's efforts led the Kiowa Street YWCA building in Colorado Springs — which she had personally led fundraising to build — to be turned over free of charge as an emergency hospital. Hundreds received care there during the worst weeks of the epidemic's local impact. The pivot from women's residence to public hospital during a public-health emergency was the most consequential single decision of her thirteen-year YWCA presidency.

narrative_blocks.header: Colorado Springs YWCA president thirteen years
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Terry Brinley served as president of the Colorado Springs YWCA from 1908 through 1922 — thirteen years that included not just the wartime flu crisis but the long-planned Kiowa Street building fundraising. She resigned the presidency in December 1922 due to ill health and died of pneumonia in 1931. Prior to her presidency she had been a YWCA board director.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Woman's Educational Society, St. Stephens parish
narrative_blocks.body: Ellen Brinley's civic portfolio also included co-founding the Woman's Educational Society of Colorado College — which funded a girls' dormitory — and founding St. Stephens Episcopal parish. She was also a director of Associated Charities, the Colorado Springs Public Library, the Coburn Library Book Club, and the El Paso County Humane Society. The complete board portfolio spanned the women's education, public welfare, library, and humane-society causes of early-twentieth-century Mountain West progressive philanthropy.

Ellen Terry Brinley was born January 7, 1856 in Hartford Connecticut, youngest child of George Brinley Jr. and Frances Ellen Terry. She moved to Colorado Springs in the early 1880s with sister Catherine and brother-in-law Dr. Benjamin Faneuil Dunkin Adams II, living with their family until Catherine's death in 1905, then building her own home at 15 Beverly Place in 1910 where her niece Katherine Faneuil Adams lived with her. She died unmarried.
